speaker: interviewer
text: Tape four. Ike, once the refugees landed, what arrangements were made for them to move to, move into Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, basically, once, once we got them out of the, the water, out of the ship, the main idea was to move them quickly and, and disperse them, so to speak, to a variety of areas. Because most likely than not, the British would always find out when ships come in where they come in. And, and they would find out where. And they certainly would send someone from the area. If it was-- in most cases, it was a British military-- civilian police. And, and we tried our best to, to the Haganah commanders to have whatever vehicles were available to us. Again, in such a community as Nahariya where there was about 1,200 people, there was not that many cars, private cars, or-- there was buses, there was trucks, there was some motorcycles, et cetera, et cetera, and, and several of the homes of the natives of Nahariya. And we would bring people into different homes to hide them overnight. And then at the following morning, we would bring them to the station, to the bus station-- again, to trucks, and go to a variety of areas, if it was to a kibbutz. In most cases, many kibbutzim were used, because you wouldn't see the police going to a kibbutz-- very, very rare. The British police would not do that. But we did it very quickly. In fact, we had in our-- when we unloaded the Aliyah, there was a refugee that we brought to our home. At the time, my wife was-- Miriam was pregnant. And it was in the ninth month, I believe. And we brought a refugee. One was upstairs with our neighbor, Mr. Cohn-- C-O-H-N. And one was in my, in my apartment. We had two-room apartment-- a bedroom, so to speak, dining room, and the kitchen. And we had the guy under the bed. And, and lo and behold, in the early hours of the morning, there was a knock at the door. And a British patrol, police patrol, with, with-- they used to go with the army as well many times. They wanted-- they asked us if we had any-- seen any activity with illegal immigrants. And the answer, of course, was no. But in this case, I said, my wife is, s pregnant in the ninth month. And we certainly don't have any, any time to, to these type of activities. And they saw my wife she was standing there in a night gown. And he left. He was very polite. And we never had any problem. And of course, in the, in the morning, then the-- this refugee was handed over to the Sochnut. And I do not know whatever ended up with him, what did he do. But in most cases, it was, it was basic. Unload him as quickly as possible. Get him moving as quickly as possible. So by the time the authorities came in to the beach, there was an empty ship tipped on the side and no refugees. That happened with the And that happened with the The Aliyah fell apart overnight. And the entire ship was spread into an area on the seashores of probably thousands of feet. You would see parts of the boat. It just came totally apart. It was made out of wood. And-- but they were fortunate to be able to make it to Eretz Israel alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then, of course, the refugees-- not many that came into Nahariya would stay in Nahariya. Most, they would be dispersed. But there were several Hungary and Romanian Jews that stayed in Nahariya, became friends later on. As a matter of fact, one of them was, was a boxing, my boxing partner. His name was [? Lotzi ?] [? Farkash. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: And he became a general contractor. And basically, that-- our assistance to that was to help him at times with the language. But primarily, the, the Jewish Sochnut, the Jewish Agency, provided a tremendous amount of help by means of teaching him. Teaching them Hebrew so they could communicate. Because communication was always a barrier. You know, with so many languages, it would be difficult. And so, so the Jewish Agency had, you might call it not a school, but I can't remember exactly the name-- ulpan. They had many, many ulpans everywhere. So within a few days, a few weeks, you could speak Hebrew, at least the necessary Hebrew that-- to communicate. So basically, that's where we are with the immigrants.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where there times that the Haganah ended up in situations that would become very dangerous or very volatile in bringing in guns or your underground activities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were, there were such times. The, the Haganah agents in-- there were, obviously, agents throughout Europe and other countries. And they used to make the arrangements to bring in all kinds of arms and ammunitions. In, in one instance that I know of was by bringing arms hidden into these highway roller trucks, you know, that after they put the asphalt down and you pave with this huge rollers? Well, they used to fill these huge rollers with arms and ammunitions. That was before the war. After the war, as the war started, of course, the largest shipment of arms came from Czechoslovakia. And, and thanks to that, thanks to God to that. Because without that, we were-- we did not have any, much arms at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you involved in any convoys or any situations where they were bringing arms in that-- where you had conflicts?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was involved with the convoys between Nahariya and, and Haifa and Kiryat Bialik and Kiryat Motzkin. And-- but these convoys did not consist of bringing arms. These were convoys that simply because there had been instances where the Arabs had, if, if you caught a single car or truck or motorcycle, people were killed, all right?

speaker: interviewer
text: So in this case, we used to get-- gather the, the buses and the trucks and delivery trucks and whatever it is and wait until the majority of these trucks get together. And then in those cars then there would be a Haganah escort. And in most, in most cases armed with a-- the lieutenant would be armed only.

speaker: interviewee
text: Many of the girls, the women, play a great role in that. Because they used to hide also arms with them-- hand grenades or small, small firearms. And on the, on the way to Haifa, on the way to Nahariya, we used to bring supplies-- obviously, food supplies and whatever supplies needed. And people that worked in, in Haifa used to travel by convoys. I traveled many, many times by convoys, many. I was late in one convoy and two convoys, as a matter of fact. And in here, I did help some of the Hungarian refugees that had been unloaded some time earlier. I was leaving Kiryat Motzkin towards Haifa, towards Nahariya, also at the time that my wife was pregnant. And, and I lost the convoy. I arrived too late.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I decided to, to do it on my own. And I carried with me an Arabic kaffiyeh, a headgear. And because I was so dark as it is, and I could pass very easily for, for an Arab, at least at the time-- very dark, black hair, et cetera.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had always a gun with me, pistol, a Beretta, automatic 45 millimeter. So I put the kaffiyeh on my head. And the truck I was driving was a Volvo. It was an old Volvo, looked like an Arab truck, really. And I took these two guys, two Hungarian refugees. I said, hey-- because they, they arrived there. They wanted to go home to Nahariya. And I says, you lay flat on the inside of the truck. I covered them with boxes of vegetables. And I had some flour in the truck for the bakery and vegetables for another store, et cetera. And I took off.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the time we arrived [? Akko ?] was-- they used the Spanish donkeys, which is these-- like a fence, a barbed wire fence. And I-- they would force you to stop. But I tried to-- I used a little bit of reverse psychology, if you wish. It was a chance that you take, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had my gun in my, my, my arm. And I got my head out of the car, the truck. And I said in Arabic to the guy, [ARABIC]. You know, open the gate. Open the gate. So the guy went and opened the gate. I stepped on the accelerator. And I went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I wouldn't do that again. And you can do that when you are in your 20s. But when you get older, you, you think twice before you do that. I did it twice, once going to-- back the Nahariya, once going to Haifa when I lost a convoy. But, but it was all a little bit scary. Because I knew-- I had a friend, an electrician. He went on motorbike and that he tried to bypass [? Akko. ?] And he, he was killed on the spot by one of the, the Arabs guards, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so-- but then what happens was on, on this Haifa-Beirut highway, the main highway, the, the-- my friend, Moshe Sheineman, was working part-time at the telephone company in, in Nahariya. And he caught some kind of conversation that was a little bit strange to him. He spoke Arabic, Yiddish, and English, and so forth. And somehow another, the Haganah was waiting for-- there was the word that a Transjordan army convoy was coming from Lebanon to their main station in [INAUDIBLE] Carmel in Haifa. They had a Transjordan army, army camp there. But they did not know when. So one time, apparently Shalom Sheineman heard this conversation. Somebody called. And they notified the, the-- our commander. And then, of course, they evacuated several homes near Kiryat Motzkin and Kiryat Haim. All the houses near the main highway were evacuated. And then they, they brought in this time the Palmach, the commanders of the Haganah-- very, very well-trained, super fighters. And they were there for several days until, ultimately, here comes the Transjordan army convoy-- several trucks, cars, full with arms and ammunitions, with machine guns on the top, et cetera. They used to travel like that. They liked that. And I had-- at that time, I was, I was on my way to Nahariya to the meeting place in Kiryat Motzkin, where the convoy congregates. But I never made it there, maybe within a couple of kilometers. All of a sudden, there was these huge explosions. What happened-- one of the Palmach fighters by the name of it [? Uzi-- ?] [? Uzi ?] was his name. I don't remember his family name. He ran from his hiding into the main, main of highway, in the center of the highway. And he, he shot with the machine gun at the first Transjordan truck. And that exploded. And [? Uzi ?] was himself, was thrown out-- I don't know how many feet. And for several years, he underwent surgery. He became one of the Israeli heroes of the war at the time. And not a single car of the Arab convoy went through. They were all killed at Kiryat Motzkin at that station. By the time I arrived, the, the British police were they already. They closed the roads. But we saw, we saw what they were doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were picking up portions of bodies. And they were not touching this portion of bodies. They were pushing it into [NON-ENGLISH], into a portable-- I don't know how you call that-- like a portable when somebody is sick. What do you call this? You can lay down on it? Gurney?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: A gurney.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a portable one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or a bed. Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were pushing the parts in there. And they were next to the truck and went, one, two, three, and tossed the stuff-- the human remains-- in there. But not one man was kept alive, found alive. That was the Arab Transjordan army convoy caught by the Haganah. However, a little bit later on, the Haganah had a convoy from Nahariya to Yehi-am. My unit was in that convoy. I was supposed to be on that convoy. However, my wife was giving birth at the time. And Dr. Cohn advised me because she was going to have a very difficult birth, which she did-- 24 hours-- he asked me to stay, stay behind, not go with the convoy. That saved my life. Because our convoy was ambushed by the Arabic leader, Fawzi [? El-Kauji, ?] al-Kauji-- K-A-U-J-I-- in the, in the the roads from Nahariya to, to Yehi-am. And, and they ambushed the convoy. The-- And they killed 40, 49 members of the Haganah, my best friend, Shalom Sheineman, his brother, his sister-- no, his sister, not his brother. He died later on. And most of my entire unit, our, our group that always has to be together, was annihilated by Fawzi al-Kauji men. Because the, the, the highway from Nahariyah to, to Yehi-am was zig-zag, very zig-zaggy. The, the-- we used to call it the road of bakshish. Bakshish is an Arabic word for tip. Because when the British build that highway, the Arabs gave them a tip to make the highway go by their gates of them, of the orange groves or, or whatever groves they had. And so as a result, the [INAUDIBLE] for about five kilometers went like this. So it was very, very difficult to prevent ambushes, because you couldn't see within a few hundred feet what was on the other side. So as a result, we, we suffered. There was a revenge on the Transjordan army convoy. It was the Yehi-am convoy. Yehi-am however, was a kibbutz named after a young man by the name of Yechiam Weiss. His father was a member of the Jewish Agency. And Yechiam Weiss was a member of the Palmach, the Haganah commandos, that had been actually directed to blow a bridge in Achziv, A-C-H-Z-I-V, a little bit north of Nahariya. This was called Operation Bridges in 1947, in, in Palestine. The main reason was the Haganah wanted to show the Arabs, don't, don't mess around with us, and also to tell the British, we are going take care of ourselves. So they blew up every bridge connecting the Arab states with Israel from the north to the south throughout. Unfortunately, the the bridge of Achziv was not blown up, because the group of the Palmach that was heading their way with the, the dynamite, et cetera, was ambushed by the British. And they were never found. They, they, they were blown to pieces, never found. And there is today-- there's a memorial in Achziv for the fighters of the Palmach that fell in Achziv. So basically, that's where we are with the convoys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ike, you talked about your wife. Can you tell us when you met her and where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was a winter day. I came into Cafe Penguin in Nahariya. And it was pretty much empty. So I walked in. I saw this beautiful lady which looked like Dorothy, Dorothy Lamour-- long, dark hair and nice dress, et cetera. So I looked. There was hardly nobody in the restaurant.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I walked inside. And I said, excuse me? Do you mind if I sit by you? And she looked at me and a little bit surprised. And she said, I'm waiting for company. But I don't mind if you sit here. So I sat. And we start talking. And a little while later, her uncle which was recuperating from an illness-- was an older gentleman-- Mr. Oppenheimer, he came in. And we sit down. And we start-- I speak German as well. So we start speaking in German.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I guess he kind of liked me a little bit. And I ask to-- if I could ask Miriam to come to the dance tonight. There was a dance in there. And they said, but you must bring her back to the-- not over 11 o'clock, something like that. So that's how come I met my wife. Later on, we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1947, July 3rd. It's going to be 50 years next year, so the Lord's will, half a century.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then did you have children when you were in Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we, we, we have three children, beautiful children. One is Tamara. She was born during the war in 1948. And she lives in Switzerland. We have the two granddaughters. One is Nadia, 24. The other one is Tina, 22. And a grandson, Robi, is 14. Then I have a son, Daniel. He has two little girls, Rachel and Tami. One is 11. One is 10. And then my other son, Mike, has a son, David, by-- he's about 19, and Joy, which is about 16. We're a very nice family.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you and Miriam decide to leave Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Miriam's family came from Germany to the United States. I do not know exactly when, but sometime in the early '33 or '30, something like that. They left on vacation to Czechoslovakia. But they never returned to Germany. My, my wife's brother went to South America, to Montevideo, Uruguay. He died a few years ago there. And so Miriam's cousins, the Senator Rudy Boschwitz from Minnesota and Franz Boschwitz in Oshkosh, Wisconsin, then asked us to come and join him in the, join him the United States. Because I had no way, as a Portuguese, there were-- I had no way of coming to America. I came to America on my wife's visa. We were-- which was a German visa and an open visa. I am very, very happy and very proud to be able to be here in this beautiful country.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your reasons for leaving Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to me, my wife's family and to be with her family, because in, in Israel, of course, I had a brother and a cousin. And later on, two sisters came to Israel. And I love Israel, but I didn't have as many opportunities to advance in life. And I thought I was given an opportunity by my cousins, my wife's cousins. And I felt that it was my duty then to give my children also an opportunity to advance in life. And I think I had done my share, even though one never really fulfills really given share to the community, et cetera. But I decided that we're going to give it a try and come to America. We like America. And we're very happy to be here.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in Israel the years before you came to America, had you kept a communication going between yourself and your family in Lisbon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, always, always-- I always communicated with, primarily with my, with my Aunt Esther, because she had the most beautiful handwriting. I loved to read her letters. She read-- wrote beautiful Portuguese. And I had some letters, of course, from my father and my mother as well. But some from the family-- a large family-- but we always communicated. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have much to say about the war in Europe and what was going on there? Did they write to you about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I came to visit them about 15 years later after I had left, and I brought my wife and three children, I think we had a hero's welcome. And the whole community was very happy to see us and welcomed us, as well as my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had their situation in Lisbon remained as good as it was when you had been there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Perhaps a little bit better, but not by much, my immediate family-- some, some of my family was. My sisters, they married not within the faith. Because there was no Jewish youth in Portugal. And that was one of the main reasons, too, that I left. And, and my brothers also married not within the faith. And I think that I'm very happy, ultimately, that I was able to, to marry within my faith-- that's what I wanted-- and raise, raise Jewish children, which I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your employment in Israel after the war, before you came to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: I became a tourist driver guide. As a matter of fact, I drove the president of Israel, Isaac Ben-Zvi, to the excavations of Hatzor, H-A-T-Z-O-R, when they were excavating. And in Rosh Pina, I picked up a very famous person-- the-- at the time, was the chief of the staff of the Israeli army and also a leading archeologist [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] by the name of-- God, I have a mental block right now. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Give me a moment so my, my thoughts come back. But we went to Hatzor. And, and I also drove. No, I was an escort for President Chaim Weizmann to the kibbutz Yam during the Second Armistice, no. And I was also an escort from the Haganah to another, another general [INAUDIBLE], General [? Drawer, ?] I believe, to the American kibbutz in Gesher HaZiv, the Bridge of Glory, that was named after these [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any experience driving anyone from Poland or from any of the countries that had--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I drove a professor which was a guest of the government of Israel from Haifa to the King David in Jerusalem. And many years later, I found out who the man was. I, I did not know his name at the time. But his name was Fritz [INAUDIBLE], who was the leading primary person to work on the discovery of aspirin. He was a Jewish man from Austria. As a matter of fact, I was asked to-- because I spoke German-- to drive a German couple to, to Jerusalem. But when I came to pick them up at the Hotel Zion in Haifa, they were speaking Portuguese. They were speaking Brazilian.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I kind of didn't say much of anything, just enjoyed listening to their comments about what they like and what they dislikes. And ultimately, I gave in and said, listen. I speak Portuguese. It's my mother language. And they said, only in Israel that can happen.
